assertions.reason: I found only partial read-only coverage: one widget flow proves a read-only mode change disables the turn submit and git pull buttons on an open thread, but there is no targeted flutter-mobile-widget-tests case that exercises the full read-only matrix from the contract (viewing while blocking steer, interrupt, approve, reject, push, pull, and branch switch).

Control-with-approvals mode did permit normal turn control: an idle thread could start a turn, and a running thread could steer and interrupt it. But dangerous git actions were not approval-gated from that same mode: branch switch, pull, and push all executed immediately with success feedback instead of stopping for explicit approval.

The approvals widget harness showed the mode split clearly: full-control mode exposed an actionable Approve button, while the lower-permission mode kept Approve and Reject disabled with a user-facing only-in-full-control message.
The mobile unpair flow passed by navigating through device settings, tapping Unpair, clearing trusted bridge/session state, calling revokeTrust once, and returning the app to the fresh pairing UI.
The settings surface passed while showing the paired bridge identity, Connected state, current access mode, notification preferences, and visible security-event details.

Changing access mode from settings succeeded and had immediate visible effect: the settings surface issued the bridge policy update for Read-only, and the already-open thread detail surface immediately disabled turn and git controls.

The available widget coverage is split between a settings page that shows/toggles notification preferences and a separate runtime-notification surface that shows a global banner, but there is no targeted flutter-mobile-widget-tests flow that changes a notification preference and then proves delivered-versus-suppressed runtime behavior through the visible UI.

The settings/device-management surface exposed recent security activity with enough detail to identify the actor, the target policy field, and the allowed outcome for the sensitive access-mode change event.

frictions: The flutter-mobile-widget-tests surface provides command logs rather than screenshots or videos, so this report relies on saved Flutter widget-test logs for user-surface evidence.

The assigned access-mode assertions are split across settings, threads, approvals, and pairing harnesses rather than a single device-management flow, so I had to combine multiple targeted widget flows to evaluate them.

blockers.description: No targeted flutter-mobile-widget-tests flow exercises the complete read-only access-mode contract across all blocked mutating controls while preserving the viewing surfaces.
blockers.quickFixAttempted: Searched the existing settings, pairing, threads, and approvals widget harnesses and ran the closest read-only gating flow plus the lower-permission approvals flow; neither covers the full read-only control matrix from the contract.

blockers.description: No targeted flutter-mobile-widget-tests flow ties a settings notification toggle change to delivered-versus-suppressed runtime notification banners in the visible UI.
blockers.quickFixAttempted: Ran the settings surface test and the runtime notification surface test, then reviewed the existing settings harnesses; only controller-level tests cover suppression/re-enable behavior after preference changes.
